idioms.definition: Used to introduce a new topic or a piece of additional information that the speaker has just thought of
examples: By the way, have you heard that Sara is moving to London?
I enjoyed the meal. By the way, can I have the recipe?
By the way, the meeting has been moved to Thursday.
idioms.usageTip: Use this to change the subject or add something you almost forgot to mention. It is very common in informal conversation.

idioms.definition: Alone; without help from anyone else
examples: She learned to cook on her own by watching videos online.
Can you fix this on your own, or do you need help?
He prefers to work on his own rather than in a team.
idioms.usageTip: Can mean physically alone OR doing something without assistance. The context usually makes it clear which meaning is intended.

idioms.definition: Finally; after a long time or after a lot of events; as a final result
examples: We argued a lot, but in the end we agreed on a price.
In the end, she decided not to take the job.
It was a difficult project, but in the end it was worth it.
idioms.usageTip: Use 'in the end' to describe the final outcome after a process. Don't confuse it with 'at the end', which refers to a specific point (e.g., 'at the end of the film').

idioms.definition: Finally, after a long wait or after much difficulty; expressing relief
examples: At last, the rain has stopped!
After months of searching, he found a job at last.
At last she understood what the teacher had been trying to explain.
idioms.usageTip: Often used to express relief or satisfaction after a long wait. It has a more emotional, expressive tone than 'finally'.

idioms.definition: A few days ago; recently (used when you don't remember or don't want to specify the exact day)
examples: I ran into your sister the other day at the supermarket.
The other day, I found an old photo album in the attic.
I was talking to Tom the other day, and he mentioned you.
idioms.usageTip: This always refers to the recent past (usually within the last week or two). It makes conversation sound natural and informal.

idioms.definition: A person who spends too much time sitting or lying down, watching TV and being physically inactive
examples: He used to be a real couch potato, but now he goes to the gym every day.
Don't be a couch potato – go outside and get some fresh air!
I was such a couch potato last weekend – I watched six episodes in a row.
idioms.usageTip: This is a slightly humorous, informal expression. It is not usually offensive, but be careful using it directly to someone as it could sound rude.

fillInExercises.sentence: ___, I saw a really good documentary about climate change.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'The other day' means a few days ago, used when you don't specify the exact day.

fillInExercises.sentence: She's a total ___. She watches TV for hours and never exercises.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Couch potato' describes someone who is very inactive and spends lots of time watching TV.

fillInExercises.sentence: The negotiations went on for weeks, but ___ they reached an agreement.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'In the end' means finally, after a long process.

fillInExercises.sentence: ___, I forgot to mention – your package arrived this morning.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'By the way' is used to introduce a new topic or something you almost forgot to say.

fillInExercises.sentence: The taxi is here ___! We've been waiting for forty minutes.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'At last' expresses relief that something has finally happened after a long wait.

fillInExercises.sentence: Did you build that bookshelf ___, or did someone help you?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On your own' means without help from anyone else.

lines.speaker: Nina
lines.text: I'm exhausted. I spent the whole evening on the sofa watching TV.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: You're turning into a real [couch potato|couch potato]! You should come to the gym with me.

lines.speaker: Nina
lines.text: I know, I know. [By the way|By the way], how did your job interview go?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: It went well! [In the end|In the end], I think they really liked me.

lines.speaker: Nina
lines.text: That's great! Did they say when they'll let you know?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: By Friday hopefully. I also met an old classmate [the other day|the other day] – he works for the same company!

lines.speaker: Nina
lines.text: What a coincidence! Did you recognise him straight away?

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Not at first. But [at last|at last] it clicked and we had a great catch-up.

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: I've been thinking about going on a solo trip this summer.

lines.speaker: Ryan
lines.text: Really? Travelling completely [on your own|on your own]? That sounds adventurous!

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: Yes! I've always wanted to do it. I nearly booked last year, but didn't.

lines.speaker: Ryan
lines.text: [In the end|In the end], I think solo travel teaches you a lot about yourself.

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: Exactly. [By the way|By the way], do you know any good travel apps?

lines.speaker: Ryan
lines.text: I was talking to a friend [the other day|the other day] who recommended one called TripIt.

lines.speaker: Lucy
lines.text: I'll look it up. I want to get this booked [at last|at last] – I've been planning this for two years!

lines.speaker: Ryan
lines.text: Go for it! Just don't become a [couch potato|couch potato] and change your mind at the last minute.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: used to introduce additional or unrelated information
finally, as a result after a long process
finally, after a long wait (expressing relief)
a lazy, physically inactive person who watches a lot of TV

quizExercises.sentence: I bumped into your brother ___ outside the coffee shop.
quizExercises.explanation: 'The other day' refers to a recent but unspecified day in the past.

quizExercises.sentence: ___ we missed the flight, so we had to book a hotel for the night.
quizExercises.explanation: 'In the end' means as a final result or outcome.

quizExercises.question: Which situation best matches 'at last'?
options: You finish a task quickly and easily.
You finally receive news you have been waiting weeks for.
You introduce a new topic in a conversation.
quizExercises.explanation: 'At last' expresses relief after a long wait. It is not used for quick or easy situations.

quizExercises.question: When would you say 'by the way'?
options: When you want to describe the route you took
When you want to add a new piece of information to the conversation
When something finally happens after a long delay
quizExercises.explanation: 'By the way' is used to introduce additional or unrelated information during a conversation.
